text: The obturator foramen is the largest foramen in the human body. Though under normal conditions, all but the most superior aspect is covered by a sturdy fibrous membrane. The resulting defect is known as the obturator canal, a tunnel that allows passage of the obturator artery, vein, and nerve between the pelvis and the anterior thigh. Herniation of abdominal contents through this space is referred to as an obturator hernia. Any physiologic scenario involving a widened pelvis or laxity of the obturator membrane increase the chances of herniation. Relevant risk factors therefore include female sex, advanced age, and malnutrition.

As these hernias are rarely noticed as masses by the patient, the vast majority are discovered only upon incarceration of abdominal viscera with consequent obstructive symptoms. Our patient, a seventy-five-year-old female, presented to the emergency room from home complaining of crampy abdominal pain for two days. Described as intermittent, severe, and poorly localized, she also noted seven episodes of non-bilious vomiting and had not passed stool or flatus in the previous forty-eight hours. Past medical history significant for atrial fibrillation, for which she took aspirin. Laboratory results were only significant for a leukocytosis of fifteen point eight.

CT imaging reveals a dilated small bowel and collapsed colon, with a transition point at the superior pubic ramus. A loop of small bowel and hernia sac are visibly protruding through the obturator canal, situated between the pectineus and obturator externus muscles.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic approach with modified Hassan entry at Palmer's point. General retraction is applied to reduce the incarcerated bowel segment, which shows bruising but appears viable. The superior pubic ramus is visible as a highly reflective structure above the hernia. Further retraction allows reduction of the hernia sac.

The video shows bilateral indirect inguinal hernias, with the left hernia being wider and immediately visible upon entry into the abdomen. The right hernia is discovered after reducing the obturator hernia. The surgeon converts to a total extraperitoneal approach, revealing a fat-containing right-sided obturator hernia. The superior pubic ramus is visible above the incarcerated fat, and the round ligament crosses under the grasper to the right. The surgeon reduces the incarcerated fat using blunt dissection and counter-traction, clearing adipose and areolar tissue on the right side. As the anatomy is defined, the pubic ramus becomes clearer, and the iliac artery and vein are exposed to the right of the round ligament. The cleared space over both inguinal and obturator hernias is demonstrated, providing adequate room for mesh placement.

The surgeon focuses on the left side, identifying the original obturator hernia near the superior pubic ramus. The hernia sac is grasped and reduced using general traction. Blunt dissection and counter traction are applied to remove loosely adherent tissue, progressively revealing underlying anatomical structures and creating space for mesh placement.

The surgeon examines the polypropylene mesh on the left side, confirming its coverage over both inguinal and obturator hernias. A second piece of mesh is placed on the right side, and both meshes are secured with three visible tacks to the underlying pubic bone.

The preperitoneal balloon is deflated, allowing the mesh to fall into place within the surgical field. The camera is reinserted into the peritoneal cavity, revealing the mesh positioned correctly with the peritoneum intact and no mesh exposure. Fat contents from the hernia are visible between the mesh and the peritoneum. A final examination of the bowel confirms its viability.
